edges.description: Defective acylcarnitine translocation limits matrix substrate delivery for long-chain FAO.
edges.target: Impaired mitochondrial long-chain fatty acid oxidation

edges.description: Failure of long-chain fatty acid oxidation is reflected by long-chain acylcarnitine accumulation and reduced free carnitine.
edges.source: Impaired mitochondrial long-chain fatty acid oxidation
edges.target: Toxic acylcarnitine accumulation and secondary carnitine depletion

edges.description: Fasting, illness, and the neonatal birth transition increase dependence on long-chain FAO, exposing the energy-generation block.
intermediate_mechanisms: Increased reliance on long-chain fatty acids during fasting, illness, and early neonatal adaptation.
Reduced acetyl-CoA, reducing-equivalent, ATP, and ketone-body generation.
edges.source: Impaired mitochondrial long-chain fatty acid oxidation

edges.description: Impaired long-chain FAO reduces hepatic ketogenesis and forces glucose dependence during fasting or illness.
intermediate_mechanisms: Reduced mitochondrial acetyl-CoA production from long-chain fatty acids.
Impaired ketone-body synthesis with enhanced reliance on glucose oxidation.
edges.source: Impaired mitochondrial long-chain fatty acid oxidation

edges.description: Long-chain acylcarnitine/lipid accumulation and reduced fatty-acid energy production injure cardiomyocytes.
intermediate_mechanisms: Cardiomyocyte energy deficit during reliance on long-chain fatty acids.
Lipid-laden microvesicular steatosis in cardiac myocytes.
edges.source: Toxic acylcarnitine accumulation and secondary carnitine depletion

edges.description: Blocked acylcarnitine handling produces the characteristic elevated long-chain acylcarnitine profile.
edges.source: Toxic acylcarnitine accumulation and secondary carnitine depletion

edges.description: Long-chain acylcarnitine accumulation is captured clinically by elevated circulating acylcarnitines.
edges.source: Toxic acylcarnitine accumulation and secondary carnitine depletion

edges.description: Trapping of carnitine in acylcarnitine species can deplete circulating free carnitine.
edges.source: Toxic acylcarnitine accumulation and secondary carnitine depletion

edges.description: Secondary carnitine depletion is measured as low circulating free carnitine.
edges.source: Toxic acylcarnitine accumulation and secondary carnitine depletion

edges.description: Impaired mitochondrial long-chain FAO can shunt fatty acids toward omega-oxidation and urinary dicarboxylic acids.
intermediate_mechanisms: Mitochondrial long-chain FAO block.
Compensatory omega-oxidation and urinary organic acid excretion.
edges.source: Toxic acylcarnitine accumulation and secondary carnitine depletion

edges.description: Lipid accumulation and hepatic energy failure cause liver dysfunction with hepatomegaly.
intermediate_mechanisms: Hepatocyte microvesicular steatosis.
Hepatic dysfunction during metabolic decompensation.
edges.source: Toxic acylcarnitine accumulation and secondary carnitine depletion

edges.description: Hepatocyte lipid accumulation and energy failure produce hepatocellular injury reflected by transaminitis.
intermediate_mechanisms: Hepatocyte microvesicular steatosis.
Hepatic dysfunction during long-chain FAO crises.
edges.source: Toxic acylcarnitine accumulation and secondary carnitine depletion

edges.description: Toxic lipid accumulation and energy failure in skeletal muscle predispose to muscle breakdown during stress.
intermediate_mechanisms: Rhabdomyocyte microvesicular steatosis.
Skeletal muscle energy deficit during physiologic stress.
edges.source: Toxic acylcarnitine accumulation and secondary carnitine depletion

edges.description: Acute FAO failure during catabolic crises contributes to hyperammonemia, which is prominent and difficult to treat in CACT deficiency.
intermediate_mechanisms: Acute hepatic energy deficit during catabolism.
Secondary disruption of nitrogen disposal during metabolic crises.

edges.description: Acute energy failure in neonatal and illness-triggered crises produces feeding difficulty.
intermediate_mechanisms: Acute systemic energy deficit during metabolic decompensation.

edges.description: Acute systemic energy deficit during metabolic crises causes decreased alertness.
intermediate_mechanisms: Acute systemic energy deficit during metabolic decompensation.

edges.description: Energy failure affecting skeletal muscle and CNS function contributes to hypotonia in the acute presentation.
intermediate_mechanisms: Skeletal muscle energy deficit.
Systemic neonatal metabolic decompensation.

edges.description: Skeletal muscle energy failure contributes to muscle weakness in severe neonatal CACT deficiency.
intermediate_mechanisms: Skeletal muscle dependence on mitochondrial fatty acid oxidation during physiologic stress.

edges.description: Severe neonatal metabolic decompensation can present with respiratory distress.
intermediate_mechanisms: Acute systemic metabolic crisis.
Cardiac, hepatic, neurologic, or respiratory muscle involvement during decompensation.

edges.description: Acute metabolic crisis and impaired energy supply can produce encephalopathy.
intermediate_mechanisms: Acute systemic metabolic decompensation.
Hyperammonemia, hypoglycemia, and impaired cerebral energy supply.

edges.description: Severe neonatal metabolic decompensation can manifest with apnea.
intermediate_mechanisms: Acute systemic metabolic decompensation.
Neurologic and respiratory instability during severe neonatal disease.

edges.description: Cardiomyocyte energy failure and lipid toxicity increase arrhythmia risk in early-onset CACT deficiency.
Energy failure and lipid accumulation in cardiomyocytes produce hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
Severe arrhythmogenesis and cardiac energy failure can culminate in cardiac arrest.
intermediate_mechanisms: Prominent early-onset cardiac arrhythmia.

edges.description: The metabolic-crisis mechanism is clinically measured as elevated blood ammonia.
Severe hypoglycemia and hyperammonemia during CACTD crises can be accompanied by seizures.
intermediate_mechanisms: Acute metabolic decompensation with hypoglycemia and hyperammonemia.
Cerebral energy failure and hyperammonemic neurotoxicity.

edges.description: Recurrent hyperammonemia and acute brain injury can lead to developmental delay or intellectual disability.
intermediate_mechanisms: Hyperammonemic brain injury during early-onset crises.
Recurrent hyperammonemia after presentation.

edges.target: Impaired mitochondrial long-chain fatty acid oxidation

edges.description: Elevated C16, C18, and C18:1 acylcarnitines report the long-chain acylcarnitine accumulation caused by defective CACT-mediated transport.
edges.source: Toxic acylcarnitine accumulation and secondary carnitine depletion

edges.description: Low free carnitine reports the secondary carnitine-depletion component of the CACT acylcarnitine trapping mechanism.
edges.source: Toxic acylcarnitine accumulation and secondary carnitine depletion

edges.description: Increased long-chain acylcarnitine ratio indices report the abnormal acylcarnitine accumulation pattern caused by CACT deficiency and improve newborn-screening discrimination.

edges.description: Dicarboxylic aciduria is a urinary organic-acid readout of impaired mitochondrial long-chain fatty acid oxidation and compensatory omega-oxidation.

evidence.reference_title: Increased acylcarnitine ratio indices in newborn screening for carnitine-acylcarnitine translocase deficiency shows increased sensitivity and reduced false-positivity.
evidence.snippet: The ratios of the primary markers (C16 + C18:1)/C2, C16/C2, C16:1/C3, and C16:1-OH/C3 can facilitate the diagnosis of CACT deficiency, thereby increasing sensitivity and reducing false-positivity.

readouts.interpretation: Increased long-chain acylcarnitine ratio indices report the abnormal acylcarnitine accumulation pattern caused by CACT deficiency and improve newborn-screening discrimination.
readouts.target: Toxic acylcarnitine accumulation and secondary carnitine depletion

nodes.description: Severe neonatal presentations can include apnea as part of respiratory and neurologic instability during metabolic crisis.

evidence.reference_title: Inborn Errors of Metabolism with Myopathy: Defects of Fatty Acid Oxidation and the Carnitine Shuttle System.
evidence.snippet: Because neonates depend largely on metabolism of long-chain fatty acids for energy, neonatal presentation is typically severe, with hypoketotic hypoglycemia, hyperammonemia, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and/or arrhythmia, apnea, hepatic dysfunction, skeletal muscle weakness, and encephalopathy.

nodes.description: In silico structural modeling demonstrates that Asp231His disrupts the matrix salt-bridge network and Ala281Val impairs helix packing and conformational transitions required for substrate transport.

evidence.reference_title: In Silico Analysis of the Structural Dynamics and Substrate Recognition Determinants of the Human Mitochondrial Carnitine/Acylcarnitine SLC25A20 Transporter.
evidence.snippet: analysis of the MD simulations' trajectories of the apo-protein in the two conformational states allowed for a better understanding of the role of SLC25A20 Asp231His and Ala281Val pathogenic mutations, which are at the basis of Carnitine-Acylcarnitine Translocase Deficiency.

nodes.description: Cardiac arrest can occur in severe early-onset disease with prominent arrhythmia.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-Acylcarnitine Translocase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Hyperammonemia and cardiac arrhythmia are prominent in early-onset disease, with high rates of cardiac arrest.

nodes.description: Cardiac arrhythmias are prominent in early-onset disease and are associated with high rates of cardiac arrest. Arrhythmia is a major contributor to early mortality.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-Acylcarnitine Translocase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Hyperammonemia and cardiac arrhythmia are prominent in early-onset disease, with high rates of cardiac arrest.

evidence.reference_title: Inborn Errors of Metabolism with Myopathy: Defects of Fatty Acid Oxidation and the Carnitine Shuttle System.
evidence.snippet: Fatty acid oxidation disorders (FAODs) and carnitine shuttling defects are inborn errors of energy metabolism with associated mortality and morbidity due to cardiomyopathy, exercise intolerance, rhabdomyolysis, and liver disease

nodes.description: The heart is highly dependent on long-chain fatty acid oxidation for energy. CACT deficiency causes cardiac energy deficit and toxic lipid accumulation in cardiomyocytes, leading to hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and cardiac arrhythmias. Cardiac involvement is often the primary determinant of mortality.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-Acylcarnitine Translocase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Hyperammonemia and cardiac arrhythmia are prominent in early-onset disease, with high rates of cardiac arrest.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-acylcarnitine translocase deficiency: experience with four cases in Spain and review of the literature.
evidence.snippet: The mortality rate is high (65%), most in the first year of life due to myocardiopathy or sudden death.

evidence.reference_title: Mitochondrial Fatty Acid Oxidation Disorders: Laboratory Diagnosis, Pathogenesis, and the Complicated Route to Treatment.
evidence.snippet: Patients with a defect in the oxidation of long-chain FAs are at risk to develop cardiac and skeletal muscle abnormalities including cardiomyopathy and arrhythmias, which may progress into early death

nodes.description: L-carnitine supplementation at approximately 100 mg/kg/day is recommended to replenish depleted free carnitine stores and support acylcarnitine excretion.

evidence.reference_title: Inborn Errors of Metabolism with Myopathy: Defects of Fatty Acid Oxidation and the Carnitine Shuttle System.
evidence.snippet: Carnitine supplementation may be helpful.

nodes.description: Birth transition, fasting, illness, or poor intake increase dependence on long-chain fatty acid oxidation. In CACT deficiency this exposes the transport and FAO block, producing acute systemic decompensation with poor feeding, lethargy, hypotonia, respiratory distress or apnea, and encephalopathy.

biological_processes: generation of precursor metabolites and energy

evidence.reference_title: Inborn Errors of Metabolism with Myopathy: Defects of Fatty Acid Oxidation and the Carnitine Shuttle System.
evidence.snippet: Because neonates depend largely on metabolism of long-chain fatty acids for energy, neonatal presentation is typically severe, with hypoketotic hypoglycemia, hyperammonemia, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and/or arrhythmia, apnea, hepatic dysfunction, skeletal muscle weakness, and encephalopathy.

nodes.description: Secondary free carnitine depletion accompanies long-chain acylcarnitine accumulation in CACT deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Inborn Errors of Metabolism with Myopathy: Defects of Fatty Acid Oxidation and the Carnitine Shuttle System.
evidence.snippet: Free carnitine is low in blood, with marked elevations of C16, C18, and C18:1 carnitine species.

evidence.reference_title: Inborn Errors of Metabolism with Myopathy: Defects of Fatty Acid Oxidation and the Carnitine Shuttle System.
evidence.snippet: Urine organic acids may show dicarboxylic aciduria.

readouts.interpretation: Dicarboxylic aciduria is a urinary organic-acid readout of impaired mitochondrial long-chain fatty acid oxidation and compensatory omega-oxidation.
readouts.target: Impaired mitochondrial long-chain fatty acid oxidation

nodes.description: The diagnostic acylcarnitine profile shows elevated long-chain species, especially C16, C18, and C18:1.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-Acylcarnitine Translocase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Characteristic elevation of long-chain acylcarnitines C16, C18, and C18:1 on acylcarnitine profile suggests a diagnosis of CACT or CPT II deficiency.

nodes.description: Transaminitis reflecting hepatocellular injury and hepatic dysfunction is a typical feature of CACT deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-Acylcarnitine Translocase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Other clinical features are typical for disorders of long-chain fatty acid oxidation: poor feeding, lethargy, hypoketotic hypoglycemia, hypotonia, transaminitis, liver dysfunction with hepatomegaly, and rhabdomyolysis.

nodes.description: Acute management centers on reversal of catabolism with high-rate intravenous dextrose infusion (12-15 g/kg/day for infants, 10-12 g/kg/day for children). Ammonia scavenger medications are of limited efficacy. Cardiac arrhythmias, cardiomyopathy, rhabdomyolysis, and acute renal impairment are treated per standard of care, typically in the ICU. Triheptanoin should be considered for cardiogenic shock.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-Acylcarnitine Translocase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Hyperammonemia is most sensitive to high rates of dextrose infusion (12-15 g/kg/day glucose for infants and 10-12 g/kg/day for children age 1-6 years), while ammonia scavenging medications (sodium benzoate, sodium phenylbutyrate) are of limited efficacy.

nodes.description: Severe neonatal metabolic decompensation can include encephalopathy due to combined energy failure, hypoglycemia, and hyperammonemia.

evidence.reference_title: Inborn Errors of Metabolism with Myopathy: Defects of Fatty Acid Oxidation and the Carnitine Shuttle System.
evidence.snippet: Because neonates depend largely on metabolism of long-chain fatty acids for energy, neonatal presentation is typically severe, with hypoketotic hypoglycemia, hyperammonemia, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and/or arrhythmia, apnea, hepatic dysfunction, skeletal muscle weakness, and encephalopathy.

nodes.description: Prevention of metabolic crises through strict avoidance of prolonged fasting, provision of nocturnal feeding, and implementation of a home emergency plan for prompt illness management. Pre-procedure hospital management with IV dextrose is recommended for surgeries or sedation.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-Acylcarnitine Translocase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: A home emergency plan for prompt illness management should be provided to parents, primary care providers, teachers, and school staff.

evidence.reference_title: Nutritional Management of Patients with Fatty Acid Oxidation Disorders.
evidence.snippet: Treatment of fatty acid oxidation disorders is based on dietary, pharmacological and metabolic decompensation measures. It is essential to provide the patient with sufficient glucose to prevent lipolysis

evidence.reference_title: Increased acylcarnitine ratio indices in newborn screening for carnitine-acylcarnitine translocase deficiency shows increased sensitivity and reduced false-positivity.
evidence.snippet: The second category for patients P7 and P8 showed a significant decrease in the C0 level and a normal concentration of long-chain acylcarnitines.

readouts.interpretation: Low free carnitine reports the secondary carnitine-depletion component of the CACT acylcarnitine trapping mechanism.
readouts.target: Toxic acylcarnitine accumulation and secondary carnitine depletion

nodes.description: Affected individuals with early-onset disease typically experience brain injury at presentation and recurrent hyperammonemia, leading to developmental delay and intellectual disability. Later-onset cases have better developmental outcomes.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-Acylcarnitine Translocase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Affected individuals with early-onset disease typically experience brain injury at presentation, and have recurrent hyperammonemia leading to developmental delay / intellectual disability.

nodes.description: Liver enlargement with hepatic dysfunction, transaminitis, and microvesicular steatosis reflecting impaired mitochondrial fat handling and lipid accumulation in hepatocytes.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-Acylcarnitine Translocase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Other clinical features are typical for disorders of long-chain fatty acid oxidation: poor feeding, lethargy, hypoketotic hypoglycemia, hypotonia, transaminitis, liver dysfunction with hepatomegaly, and rhabdomyolysis.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-acylcarnitine translocase deficiency: experience with four cases in Spain and review of the literature.
evidence.snippet: the most frequent presenting symptoms of CACT deficiency are hypoketotic hypoglycemia, hyperammonemia, hepatomegaly, cardiomyopathy and/or arrhythmia, and respiratory distress.

nodes.description: The mainstay of chronic therapy is a high-carbohydrate diet providing over 60% of total caloric intake, with restriction of long-chain dietary fat to less than 10% of total calories. Fasting is avoided or strictly limited. Essential fatty acid targets include linoleic acid at 3-4% and linolenic acid at 0.5-1% of total calories.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-Acylcarnitine Translocase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: The mainstay of therapy is a high-carbohydrate diet (>60% of total caloric intake) with restriction of long-chain dietary fat (to <10% of total calories)

evidence.reference_title: Nutritional Management of Patients with Fatty Acid Oxidation Disorders.
evidence.snippet: In long-chain deficits, long-chain triglyceride restriction should be 10% of total energy, with linoleic acid and linolenic acid intake of 3-4% and 0.5-1%

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-acylcarnitine translocase deficiency: experience with four cases in Spain and review of the literature.
evidence.snippet: Diagnosis before the occurrence of clinical symptoms by tandem MS-MS and very early therapeutic intervention together with good dietary compliance could lead to a better prognosis

nodes.description: Elevated ammonia is a prominent and difficult-to-treat feature, particularly in early-onset disease. It is an important determinant of neurocognitive outcome.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-Acylcarnitine Translocase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Hyperammonemia and cardiac arrhythmia are prominent in early-onset disease, with high rates of cardiac arrest.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-acylcarnitine translocase deficiency: experience with four cases in Spain and review of the literature.
evidence.snippet: the most frequent presenting symptoms of CACT deficiency are hypoketotic hypoglycemia, hyperammonemia, hepatomegaly, cardiomyopathy and/or arrhythmia, and respiratory distress.

nodes.description: Hyperammonemia is a prominent and difficult-to-treat feature of CACT deficiency, particularly during neonatal and acute metabolic crises. It is an important determinant of long-term neurocognitive outcome. Ammonia scavenger medications are of limited efficacy; high-rate dextrose infusion is the most effective intervention.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-Acylcarnitine Translocase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Hyperammonemia is difficult to treat and is an important determinant of long-term neurocognitive outcome.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-acylcarnitine translocase deficiency: experience with four cases in Spain and review of the literature.
evidence.snippet: the most frequent presenting symptoms of CACT deficiency are hypoketotic hypoglycemia, hyperammonemia, hepatomegaly, cardiomyopathy and/or arrhythmia, and respiratory distress.

nodes.description: Univentricular or biventricular hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, ranging from mild to severe, is a common feature. Cardiac involvement may respond to dietary and medical therapies but is a frequent cause of death.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-Acylcarnitine Translocase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Univentricular or biventricular hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, ranging from mild to severe, may respond to appropriate dietary and medical therapies.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-acylcarnitine translocase deficiency: experience with four cases in Spain and review of the literature.
evidence.snippet: the most frequent presenting symptoms of CACT deficiency are hypoketotic hypoglycemia, hyperammonemia, hepatomegaly, cardiomyopathy and/or arrhythmia, and respiratory distress.

nodes.description: Fasting intolerance with low ketone production and hypoglycemia is a hallmark of CACT deficiency, resulting from the inability to oxidize long-chain fatty acids for ketogenesis.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-Acylcarnitine Translocase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Other clinical features are typical for disorders of long-chain fatty acid oxidation: poor feeding, lethargy, hypoketotic hypoglycemia, hypotonia, transaminitis, liver dysfunction with hepatomegaly, and rhabdomyolysis.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-acylcarnitine translocase deficiency: experience with four cases in Spain and review of the literature.
evidence.snippet: the most frequent presenting symptoms of CACT deficiency are hypoketotic hypoglycemia, hyperammonemia, hepatomegaly, cardiomyopathy and/or arrhythmia, and respiratory distress.

evidence.reference_title: Mitochondrial Fatty Acid Oxidation Disorders: Laboratory Diagnosis, Pathogenesis, and the Complicated Route to Treatment.
evidence.snippet: A general characteristic of all mFAO disorders is hypoketotic hypoglycemia resulting from the enhanced reliance on glucose oxidation and the inability to synthesize ketone bodies from FAs.

nodes.description: Impaired transport of long-chain acylcarnitines into the mitochondrial matrix blocks long-chain fatty acid beta-oxidation. This leads to inadequate ATP generation during fasting, birth, or illness, and impaired ketogenesis, shifting energy reliance to glucose until glycogen stores are depleted.
nodes.id: Impaired mitochondrial long-chain fatty acid oxidation

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-Acylcarnitine Translocase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: CACT deficiency causes a defect in mitochondrial long-chain fatty acid β-oxidation, with variable clinical severity.

nodes.description: Decreased alertness and lethargy during acute metabolic decompensation episodes, reflecting systemic energy failure.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-Acylcarnitine Translocase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Other clinical features are typical for disorders of long-chain fatty acid oxidation: poor feeding, lethargy, hypoketotic hypoglycemia, hypotonia, transaminitis, liver dysfunction with hepatomegaly, and rhabdomyolysis.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-Acylcarnitine Translocase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Characteristic elevation of long-chain acylcarnitines C16, C18, and C18:1 on acylcarnitine profile suggests a diagnosis of CACT or CPT II deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Increased acylcarnitine ratio indices in newborn screening for carnitine-acylcarnitine translocase deficiency shows increased sensitivity and reduced false-positivity.
evidence.snippet: The acylcarnitine profiles from 15 patients were classified into three categories using C12, C14, C16, C18, C16:1, C18:1, and C18:2 as the primary diagnostic markers.

readouts.interpretation: Elevated C16, C18, and C18:1 acylcarnitines report the long-chain acylcarnitine accumulation caused by defective CACT-mediated transport.
readouts.target: Toxic acylcarnitine accumulation and secondary carnitine depletion

nodes.description: MCT oil (10-30% of total calories) provides alternative fuel that bypasses the long-chain fatty acid transport defect. MCT oil is used as a substitute when triheptanoin is not available.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-Acylcarnitine Translocase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: If triheptanoin is not available, medium-chain triglyceride (MCT) oil (10%-30% of total calories) could be used as a substitute.

nodes.description: Skeletal muscle weakness can be part of the severe neonatal CACT deficiency presentation.

evidence.reference_title: Inborn Errors of Metabolism with Myopathy: Defects of Fatty Acid Oxidation and the Carnitine Shuttle System.
evidence.snippet: Because neonates depend largely on metabolism of long-chain fatty acids for energy, neonatal presentation is typically severe, with hypoketotic hypoglycemia, hyperammonemia, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and/or arrhythmia, apnea, hepatic dysfunction, skeletal muscle weakness, and encephalopathy.

nodes.description: Hypotonia is a typical feature of CACT deficiency, reflecting impaired skeletal muscle energy metabolism.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-Acylcarnitine Translocase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Other clinical features are typical for disorders of long-chain fatty acid oxidation: poor feeding, lethargy, hypoketotic hypoglycemia, hypotonia, transaminitis, liver dysfunction with hepatomegaly, and rhabdomyolysis.

nodes.description: CACT deficiency is detectable by newborn screening via tandem mass spectrometry using acylcarnitine profiling. Elevated long-chain acylcarnitines are the primary markers. Use of ratio indices improves discrimination and reduces false-positive rates. Because CACT deficiency often presents before routine screening collection timing, early clinical vigilance is also important.

evidence.reference_title: Increased acylcarnitine ratio indices in newborn screening for carnitine-acylcarnitine translocase deficiency shows increased sensitivity and reduced false-positivity.
evidence.snippet: Newborn screening via tandem mass spectrometry (MS/MS) technology enables early diagnosis.

evidence.reference_title: Increased acylcarnitine ratio indices in newborn screening for carnitine-acylcarnitine translocase deficiency shows increased sensitivity and reduced false-positivity.
evidence.snippet: The ratios of the primary markers (C16 + C18:1)/C2, C16/C2, C16:1/C3, and C16:1-OH/C3 can facilitate the diagnosis of CACT deficiency, thereby increasing sensitivity and reducing false-positivity.

nodes.description: Feeding difficulties are common, particularly in neonatal-onset disease. Feeding tube placement and/or feeding therapy may be required.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-Acylcarnitine Translocase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Other clinical features are typical for disorders of long-chain fatty acid oxidation: poor feeding, lethargy, hypoketotic hypoglycemia, hypotonia, transaminitis, liver dysfunction with hepatomegaly, and rhabdomyolysis.

nodes.description: Respiratory distress is a frequent presenting symptom in neonatal-onset CACT deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-acylcarnitine translocase deficiency: experience with four cases in Spain and review of the literature.
evidence.snippet: the most frequent presenting symptoms of CACT deficiency are hypoketotic hypoglycemia, hyperammonemia, hepatomegaly, cardiomyopathy and/or arrhythmia, and respiratory distress.

nodes.description: Skeletal muscle breakdown due to impaired muscle energy metabolism and toxic metabolite accumulation. Rhabdomyolysis can occur during metabolic decompensation episodes.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-Acylcarnitine Translocase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Other clinical features are typical for disorders of long-chain fatty acid oxidation: poor feeding, lethargy, hypoketotic hypoglycemia, hypotonia, transaminitis, liver dysfunction with hepatomegaly, and rhabdomyolysis.

evidence.reference_title: Inborn Errors of Metabolism with Myopathy: Defects of Fatty Acid Oxidation and the Carnitine Shuttle System.
evidence.snippet: Fatty acid oxidation disorders (FAODs) and carnitine shuttling defects are inborn errors of energy metabolism with associated mortality and morbidity due to cardiomyopathy, exercise intolerance, rhabdomyolysis, and liver disease

evidence.reference_title: SLC25A20 / carnitine-acylcarnitine translocase deficiency (Definitive)
evidence.snippet: SLC25A20 | HGNC:1421 | carnitine-acylcarnitine translocase deficiency | MONDO:0008918 | AR | Definitive

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-Acylcarnitine Translocase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: The diagnosis of CACT deficiency is confirmed by identification of biallelic pathogenic variants in SLC25A20

evidence.reference_title: In Silico Analysis of the Structural Dynamics and Substrate Recognition Determinants of the Human Mitochondrial Carnitine/Acylcarnitine SLC25A20 Transporter.
evidence.snippet: The Carnitine-Acylcarnitine Carrier is a member of the mitochondrial Solute Carrier Family 25 (SLC25), known as SLC25A20, involved in the electroneutral exchange of acylcarnitine and carnitine across the inner mitochondrial membrane.

nodes.description: Biallelic SLC25A20 pathogenic variants reduce mitochondrial inner membrane carnitine-acylcarnitine translocase activity.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-Acylcarnitine Translocase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: CACT deficiency causes a defect in mitochondrial long-chain fatty acid β-oxidation, with variable clinical severity.

nodes.description: Seizures may occur in the context of metabolic decompensation, energy failure, and encephalopathy.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and molecular characteristics of carnitineacylcarnitine translocase deficiency with c.270delC and a novel c.408C>A variant.
evidence.snippet: CACTD is characterized by severe episodes of hypoglycemia and hyperammonemia, seizures, cardiomyopathy, liver dysfunction, severe neurological damage, and muscle weakness.

nodes.description: Blocked transport of long-chain acylcarnitines results in their cytoplasmic accumulation and secondary depletion of free carnitine. Accumulated long-chain acylcarnitines and acyl-CoA derivatives cause biochemical toxicity in high-energy-demand tissues including heart, skeletal muscle, liver, and kidney, manifesting as lipid-laden microvesicular steatosis across multiple organs.
nodes.id: Toxic acylcarnitine accumulation and secondary carnitine depletion

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-acylcarnitine translocase deficiency: a case report with autopsy.
evidence.snippet: We describe the autopsy pathology of a child with CACT deficiency dominantly in the form of microvesicular steatosis of the hepatocytes, renal proximal tubular epithelia, cardiac myocytes, and rhabdomyocytes.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-Acylcarnitine Translocase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Characteristic elevation of long-chain acylcarnitines C16, C18, and C18:1 on acylcarnitine profile suggests a diagnosis of CACT or CPT II deficiency.

nodes.description: Triheptanoin (UX007/Dojolvi), a synthetic medium odd-chain triglyceride, provides anaplerotic support by generating propionyl-CoA that replenishes TCA cycle intermediates via succinyl-CoA. Recommended at 25-35% of total calories. Has demonstrated rapid reversal of cardiogenic shock in a CACT deficiency case and significant reduction in hospitalizations compared with MCT oil in LC-FAOD patients.

evidence.reference_title: Triheptanoin in patients with long-chain fatty acid oxidation disorders: clinical experience in Italy.
evidence.snippet: The number of intercurrent catabolic episodes during triheptanoin treatment was significantly lower than during MCT therapy (4.3 ± 5.3 vs 22.0 ± 22.2; p = 0.034), as were the number of metabolic decompensations requiring hospitalisation (mean ± SD: 2.0 ± 2.5 vs 18.3 ± 17.7; p = 0.014)

nodes.description: A recurrent splice variant common in a Chinese cohort (14/15 patients homozygous), associated with severe early-onset disease.
Novel variant identified in a Spanish patient with severe neonatal-onset disease and fatal outcome during intercurrent infection in the first year.

evidence.reference_title: Carnitine-acylcarnitine translocase deficiency: experience with four cases in Spain and review of the literature.
evidence.snippet: Two cases with neonatal onset, carrying in homozygosity the novel variant sequences p.Gly20Asp (c.59G>A) and p.Arg179Gly (c.536A>G), died during an intercurrent infectious process in the first year of life
